identity.title: Other Motion Picture and Video Industries

lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market motion-picture film laboratories and film-preservation service firms that repeatedly provide external archives, studios, libraries, museums, government bodies, rights owners, and producers with inspection, stabilization, cleaning, repair, scanning, laboratory processing, digitization, restoration, preservation masters, access copies, and exhibition or distribution derivatives.
lens.excluded_activities: Stock-footage libraries and pure footage licensors, captive archive departments, public repositories performing work only in-house, teleproduction and postproduction services classified in 512191, production, distribution, exhibition, sound studios, mass duplication, storage-only providers, independent freelancers without a transferable operating firm, and shell entities.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers buy project-based preservation and laboratory work, collection batches, multi-phase digitization programs, restoration engagements, repeat access or exhibition derivatives, and occasional custody or managed-file services. Fees are commonly based on footage, format, condition, capture specification, labor, deliverables, storage, and special handling.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines film laboratories and preservation services with stock-footage libraries. The screen retains laboratory and preservation firms because they supply an outsourced service, and excludes footage libraries because rights and asset licensing is a different business model that would make one set of primitives incoherent.

a.bridge: BLS measures all of NAICS 512100 rather than exact 512199 and reports arts, design, entertainment, sports, and media occupations at 40.73% of employment, computer occupations at 2.68%, and office support at 6.30%. NARA describes preservation workflows requiring inspection, specialized handling, high-quality capture, managed files, and derivative creation. I map metadata, cataloging, image and audio defect detection, routine color and restoration suggestions, file naming, search, quality-control triage, derivative generation, scheduling, and administration to AI exposure, then discount physical inspection, cleaning, repair, fragile-media handling, scanner operation, chemistry, custody, calibration, archival judgment, final quality control, and work already automated. The broad motion-picture occupation mix likely overstates creative editing and understates exact-industry preservation technicians, while omitting self-employed specialists and providing no wage-weighted exact-code task data.
a.rationale: Digital restoration, metadata, search, derivative creation, and quality-control triage are exposed, but the selected lens retains a large physical, equipment-bound, standards-driven, and custody-sensitive core.
a.change_evidence: An exact-512199 wage-weighted time study of laboratory and preservation firms, separating physical preparation, scanning, repair, restoration, metadata, quality control, derivative production, project management, and administration, with current automation and accepted AI-output rates, would materially move the estimate.
caveats: OEWS covers the full 512100 motion-picture and video industry rather than the narrow laboratory and preservation lens.
The public preservation guidance describes workflows and standards but does not report labor shares.
Film laboratory, analog-video transfer, high-end restoration, and archive metadata projects have materially different task mixes.

rho.bridge: Adobe documents production tools that automate footage search, caption translation, color handling, and limited clip generation, demonstrating that AI-assisted image and media workflows are commercially available. NARA requires specialized knowledge, managed storage, high capture standards, format-specific products, and careful handling for preservation work. I translate available media AI into five-year implementation for metadata, restoration assistance, defect detection, search, and routine derivatives, then discount fragile-source risk, uncommon formats, calibration, chain of custody, archival authenticity, nondestructive handling, customer approvals, limited training data, compute requirements, and mandatory human quality control. General editing tools likely overstate readiness for preservation masters, while repeat digital derivatives and metadata work may implement faster than physical laboratory operations.
rho.rationale: Assistive AI should enter digital stages of the workflow, but preservation standards and irreversible handling risks keep adoption supervised and slower than in ordinary editing.
rho.change_evidence: A representative survey of U.S. film laboratories and preservation vendors showing production AI by workflow, format, source condition, capture specification, error and rework rates, customer approval, archival-policy constraints, verified hours saved, and system cost would materially move implementation.
caveats: Adobe's editing features are not designed as an independent measure of archival-preservation adoption.
NARA specifications describe required outcomes rather than private-vendor implementation rates.
AI adoption can improve quality or discovery without reducing labor, especially when every output still receives frame-level review.

e.rationale: Only the film-laboratory and preservation-service portion of this residual code fits the narrowed outsourced-service lens. The Census definition also includes stock-footage libraries, which are excluded as rights and asset licensors. Further discounts cover captive or public in-house facilities, storage-only entities, very small specialists without transferable operations, and firms whose normalized earnings do not fit the information-services margin used for the supplied count.
e.change_evidence: A complete roster of the supplied 512199 LMM-band firms with service revenue, footage-licensing revenue, customer type, paid staff, equipment, normalized EBITDA, public or captive status, and standalone transferability would materially move eligibility.
caveats: The exact mix of film laboratories, preservation vendors, and stock-footage libraries in the supplied count is unavailable.
The supplied n is only three firms, so classification or margin error can move eligibility substantially.
The Information Services margin bridge may not represent equipment-intensive laboratories or project-funded preservation work.

s5.rationale: Specialist laboratories can transfer through founder retirement, succession, archive-services consolidation, or acquisition of scarce equipment and expertise. The tiny eligible population, dependence on technicians and customer trust, public or mission-oriented counterparties, obsolete-format equipment, and the possibility of asset sales or closure instead of a continuing-business sale limit qualifying transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census of U.S. film laboratories and preservation vendors using broker data, trade reporting, state filings, and customer notices, coded for size, continuing operations, staff and equipment retention, and true control change, would materially move the probability.
caveats: No public exact-industry transfer rate or owner-succession series was found.
Equipment auctions, archive donations, closures, and purchases of film or footage assets are not qualifying transfers of a continuing service firm.
With very few eligible firms, idiosyncratic owner decisions dominate a population probability.

q.rationale: Project and per-footage pricing allows a specialist vendor to retain initial savings from metadata, restoration assistance, quality-control triage, and derivative automation. Scarce equipment, trusted custody, and high capture standards provide differentiation, while institutional procurement, grant budgets, competitive bids, customer demands for more restoration, software and compute costs, storage, and mandatory human review share or absorb benefits.
q.change_evidence: Matched laboratory and preservation bids, invoices, and job-cost records before and after AI deployment, including source condition, footage, specifications, realized hours, compute and storage cost, rework, price changes, procurement type, and renewal or follow-on work, would materially move retention.
caveats: No source measures the retained share of implemented AI benefit in film preservation.
Grant-funded, government, studio, museum, and private-collector customers have different procurement and price-sharing behavior.
Savings may be reinvested in higher capture quality or more manual restoration rather than appearing as margin.

d5.bridge: NARA recommends reformatted access copies, has moved toward digitizing original audiovisual content, states that specialized knowledge and equipment may require a reputable outside company, and specifies high-quality digital products for film at risk from deterioration or obsolescence. It also documents irreversible acetate deterioration and prioritization for reformatting. I translate the continuing stock of vulnerable film, access demand, restoration, and format obsolescence into roughly stable five-year constant-quality outsourced service demand, then discount finite backlogs, public and grant budget cycles, completed digitization programs, studio insourcing, scarcity of playback equipment, and substitution toward lower-cost scanning. Federal preservation needs may overstate demand among commercial customers, while the physical deterioration clock supports work that cannot simply be deferred forever.
d5.rationale: Deteriorating film and demand for preservation and access copies create a durable backlog, but funding is lumpy and each completed collection reduces remaining conversion work.
d5.change_evidence: A national inventory of undigitized motion-picture holdings by condition and format, combined with annual outsourced footage processed, real service-price deflators, funded preservation budgets, vendor backlog, utilization, project completions, and facility exits, would materially move the quantity path.
caveats: NARA guidance establishes need and workflow, not a representative commercial demand forecast.
Preservation demand depends on institutional and grant budgets and can be delayed despite physical urgency.
The narrowed lens excludes stock-footage licensing, so broader growth in video usage does not automatically raise this service quantity.

o.rationale: Fragile physical media, obsolete playback systems, specialized scanners, calibration, custody, archival authenticity, condition judgment, and high-quality capture keep an accountable operator central. Software can self-serve metadata, search, defect triage, routine enhancement, and derivative creation, and large archives may insource equipment, but most customers lacking specialized knowledge still require a laboratory or preservation operator for source handling and preservation-grade outputs.
o.change_evidence: Customer-level evidence showing which preservation and laboratory projects were insourced, fully automated, abandoned, or still outsourced after deployment of AI restoration, metadata, scanning, and quality-control systems, segmented by format, condition, customer type, and capture standard, would materially move the share.
caveats: No source directly measures year-five operator-required demand for the narrowed lens.
Routine born-digital media management is more substitutable than fragile-film handling and preservation-master creation.
Equipment simplification may enable archive insourcing even if the work still requires a human operator.

narrative.executive_view: The residual code is heterogeneous, so the coherent outsourced-service lens is film laboratory and preservation work rather than stock-footage licensing. Physical media risk and specialized capture sustain operator demand, while the tiny supplied firm count makes market availability the dominant uncertainty.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist metadata creation, catalog search, defect detection, restoration suggestions, routine color and audio cleanup, quality-control triage, derivative generation, and administration. Humans remain necessary for physical inspection, cleaning, repair, scanning, chemistry, calibration, custody, archival judgment, authenticity, and final preservation quality.
narrative.value_capture: Per-footage and project bids allow initial retention of digital-stage efficiencies. Institutional tenders, grant constraints, customer demands for higher quality, specialist software and compute, managed storage, and mandatory review push part of the benefit into price or scope.
narrative.firm_availability: The code contains only a few dataset-estimated LMM firms and mixes service providers with footage libraries. Eligibility and transfer flow are therefore fragile to classification, margin assumptions, ownership, and whether a transaction preserves a functioning specialist team and facility.
narrative.demand_durability: Deterioration, obsolete playback equipment, access needs, and preservation standards support continuing digitization and restoration. Demand remains lumpy because budgets are institutional, backlogs are finite, and some large archives can build internal capacity.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest risks are the residual-code business-model mix, a three-firm dataset estimate, the generic margin bridge, broad occupation data, scarce transaction evidence, project and grant cycles, obsolete equipment, key-person dependence, archive insourcing, and uncertain productivity from AI under preservation-grade review.
narrative.principal_driver: Fragile physical media and preservation-grade capture requirements keep specialized outsourced laboratories relevant even as digital workflow stages automate.
narrative.principal_weakness: The addressable lower-middle-market population is extremely small and classification uncertainty can remove much of it before transfer probability is considered.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual: 512199 Other Motion Picture and Video Industries
sources.supports: Exact residual-industry scope and illustrative examples of motion-picture film laboratories, stock-footage film libraries, and film-preservation services.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Motion Picture and Video Industries
sources.supports: Broader-industry occupation shares and wages used as a disclosed labor-mix proxy.

sources.title: Reformatting Audio, Video, and Motion Picture Records
sources.supports: Need for reformatting and digitization, preservation and access copies, specialized knowledge and equipment, outside vendors, and managed storage.

sources.title: Digital Output from Motion Picture Film Source
sources.supports: Preservation-grade film capture requirements, products for deteriorating or obsolete sources, restoration masters, access derivatives, and high-quality managed outputs.

sources.title: Film-Based Negatives and Positives
sources.supports: Physical film deterioration, vinegar syndrome, cold-storage and reformatting priorities, careful handling, specialist consultation, and digitization needs.

sources.title: Introducing new AI-powered features and workflow enhancements in Premiere Pro and After Effects 25.2
sources.supports: Available AI-assisted media workflows for footage search, caption translation, clip generation, and automatic color handling, used as a proxy for digital-stage automation.
